entities.name: Brimer

attributes.function: bearer of consecrated ring in resuscitation ritual

entities.raw_quote: I conjure you, O Brimer, Suburith, [etc.] ... that you should come to me now without delay, bearing the consecrated ring

entities.name: Suburith
entities.raw_quote: I conjure you, O Brimer, Suburith, [etc.] ... that you should come to me now without delay, bearing the consecrated ring

entities.name: six spirits

attributes.function: appear at circle upon first conjuration; request and depart with ring

entities.raw_quote: When you have said this once, six spirits will at once appear at the circle, requesting the ring

entities.name: spirit in horseman form

attributes.function: delivers consecrated ring; claims it is not fitting for them to come personally

entities.raw_quote: you will see toward the east someone coming on horseback, who, when he arrives at the circle, will say, 'So-and-so' - naming the names written above

entities.name: consecrated ring

attributes.function: causes living person to appear dead; revives corpse for up to six days

entities.raw_quote: whenever I place it on the finger of a dead person, one of you will enter him, and he will appear alive as before

entities.name: hoopoe

attributes.function: used to draw circle for love/fate ritual

entities.raw_quote: If you draw it on a Friday with the feather of a hoopoe and with its blood on a freshly prepared sheet of parchment

entities.name: white cloth
entities.raw_quote: Keep this ring with you, wrapped in a sheet of white cloth.

entities.name: pentagram
entities.raw_quote: The figure shown for this experiment is a double circular band with a pentagram inscribed.

entities.name: monumentum (white shroud)
entities.raw_quote: The reference to the monumentum , the white shroud, and Sunday morning before dawn all make it clear that this resuscitation is intended as a replication or perhaps a parody of Christ’s Resurrection.

entities.name: sword

attributes.function: used in conjuration; carried away after ritual

entities.raw_quote: you likewise should leave the circle, taking the sword with you, and not destroying the circle

entities.name: Oymelor, Symofos, Manoir, Faubair

attributes.rank: names on outermost band of ring

entities.raw_quote: Oymelor, Symofos, Manoir and Faubair in the outermost band

entities.name: Demefin, Rodobayl, Tamafin, Abelutabel

attributes.rank: names on second band of ring

entities.raw_quote: Demefin, Rodobayl, Tamafin and Abelutabel in the next

entities.name: Lamair, Tentetos, Leutaber, Rimasor

attributes.rank: names on third band of ring

entities.raw_quote: Lamair, Tentetos, Leutaber and Rimasor in the next

entities.name: Masair, Lotobor, Tatomofon, Sirama

attributes.rank: names on innermost band of ring

entities.raw_quote: Masair, Lotobor, Tatomofon and Sirama in the innermost

entities.name: Lantrayth / Lautrayth / Lutrayth
entities.raw_quote: The nomenclature is again variable: Lantrayth appears also as Lautrayth and Lutrayth, Feremni as Feremin and Feremim

summary: This chunk details a complex resuscitation and illusionist necromantic ritual from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), including spirit conjurations (Brimer, Suburith, six spirits, horseman demon), use of a consecrated ring to simulate death/resurrection, and associated ingredients (hoopoe blood/feather for circle drawing, white cloth for storage). It includes detailed descriptions of the ritual steps across multiple days, the structure of the magical ring with nested bands of spirit names, and theological commentary on how these practices parody Christ’s Resurrection and align with medieval views of Antichristian illusion.
key_passages: When you have said this once, six spirits will at once appear at the circle, requesting the ring, which you will give them. When you have given it, they will depart...
You will take the ring, saying to him, 'Thanks to you and to them.' When you have said this, he will at once withdraw; and you too shall depart from the circle, destroying it completely.
Keep this ring with you, wrapped in a sheet of white cloth. When you wish to cause someone to appear dead... place this ring on his finger, and he will appear to be a corpse...
The figure shown for this experiment is a double circular band with a pentagram inscribed. The names carved on the ring are repeated, along with astrological signs, within the two bands.
The reference to the monumentum , the white shroud, and Sunday morning before dawn all make it clear that this resuscitation is intended as a replication or perhaps a parody of Christ’s Resurrection.
